name: The Ground Beneath the Armistice
description: Lena Voss has found that the armistice ground is built directly above a Grid node. The coordinates she recovered from the border stone markings match the Elder People's surface-node map. She wants confirmation from the only source that would have the complete original survey: the sunken city archive. She cannot make the journey herself.
accept_text: She has the coordinates written out on a folded paper and a second paper with her analysis -- how the border stone markings decode, what coordinate system she used, the measurements she took from the ground itself. 'I need confirmation from a primary source,' she says. 'The Elder People's surface-node survey would be in the pre-Cascade records. If the archive has access to the full network now, it has access to the complete survey, not just the Foundation Quarter documentation.' She holds out the papers. 'The question I need answered: is the armistice ground a designated node site, or did it coincidentally end up above the lattice point through geographic chance.' She pauses. 'I believe it was designated. The stones that mark the armistice boundary are the Elder People's survey markers -- I identified the notation style three weeks ago. Someone placed those markers intentionally. Someone chose this ground.' She is quiet. 'If the armistice ground is a designated node site, then whoever first proposed this as the ceasefire location three hundred years ago was either an Elder People scholar who understood what the markers meant, or they felt something when they stood here that they couldn't name.' She looks at the ground. 'Either possibility changes what the armistice has been. Take the papers to the archive. Ask for the surface-node survey for this grid reference. See what it says.'
complete_text: The archive voice receives the papers. There is a pause that is longer than the archive's usual retrieval pause. Then: 'Cross-referencing provided coordinates with Foundation Grid surface-node survey, pre-Cascade full record, now accessible through restored network.' Another pause. 'Confirmation: the provided location is designated node site seven of the eastern surface array. Classification in the original survey: high-resonance intersection, recommended for extended-occupation installations that would benefit from lattice support.' A pause. 'Historical notation appended to this node entry: this site was proposed as a ceasefire location by the engineering representative to the first Armistice Committee, three hundred and twelve years ago. The representative's name is in the record. She had been a junior infrastructure engineer on the surface-node installation team before the Cascade.' The archive voice pauses again, and in the pause there is the quality of something being stated carefully. 'She knew what the ground was. She proposed it specifically because she knew that people standing on a high-resonance intersection experience reduced aggression responses and increased cooperative behavior. The Armistice of Silence has been held on a lattice node every night for three hundred years because an Elder People engineer chose this ground and knew what choosing it would do.' Another pause. 'Her name is Solen Var. She is not in the transmission-origin group. Her record ends with the notation: chose evacuation, northern route. Whether she survived is not in this archive.' A final pause. 'Please tell Researcher Voss: she was correct. The ground was chosen. The armistice was designed to hold.'
